title: The Overfitting/Underfitting Dashboard
concept: A loss-curve dashboard helps you diagnose whether a model is underfitting, overfitting, or in balance so you can choose the right next move: add data, add regularization, or change model capacity.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the clearest sign that a ReLU neuron might be dead?

options.text: It stays at zero for almost every input over time.
It activates only for some examples.
It appears in a network with more than one hidden layer.

exercises.explain: Right. Debugging often starts by spotting one symptom clearly before choosing the next action.

cards.title: A cockpit full of gauges
cards.body: Pilots do not guess from one blinking light. They read speed, altitude, and fuel together. Deep-learning diagnostics work the same way: the most useful picture comes from reading training loss and validation loss side by side.

cards.title: What the dashboard is
cards.body: The overfitting/underfitting dashboard is the habit of comparing training loss and validation loss to decide what kind of problem you have. If both stay high, the model is often underfitting. If training gets much better while validation stalls or worsens, the model is often overfitting.

cards.title: Practice score vs. game score
cards.body: If an athlete scores badly in practice and badly in games, the skill itself is missing. If practice scores become amazing but game scores stay weak, the athlete may have memorized drills instead of learning adaptable skill. That is the difference between underfitting and overfitting.

cards.title: How the dashboard guides action
cards.body: If both training and validation loss are high, try more capacity, longer training, or fewer restrictions because the model may be too weak. If training loss is low but validation loss is much higher, try more data, augmentation, or regularization. If both curves are low and close together, you are near a healthy balance.

cards.title: Low training loss is not enough
cards.body: A common myth is that very low training loss automatically means success. It does not. A model can ace the training set and still fail on new examples. Generalization lives in the gap between training and validation performance, not in training loss alone.

cards.title: Read the gap, then choose the tool
cards.body: The curves do not just describe the model. They suggest the next lever to pull.

left.text:
Training loss high, validation loss high.
Model may be too weak or too constrained.
Try more capacity or less regularization.

right.text:
Training loss low, validation loss much worse.
Model learned the training set too specifically.
Try more data, augmentation, or regularization.

exercises.prompt: If both training loss and validation loss remain high, what is the best first diagnosis?

options.text: The validation set must be corrupted.
The model is already perfectly balanced.

exercises.explain: Correct. If the model cannot do well even on training data, it usually has not learned enough capacity or flexibility yet.

exercises.prompt: True or false: A large gap where training loss is low but validation loss is much higher is a classic overfitting pattern.
exercises.explain: True. The model is doing well on seen examples but struggling to generalize.

exercises.sentence: When training loss is low but validation loss is much worse, adding more data or stronger regularization can reduce ___.
exercises.explain: Overfitting is correct. Those tools help the model rely less on quirks of the training set.

exercises.prompt: Which recommendation best matches the dashboard?

options.text: Training loss is low and validation loss is much worse, so try augmentation or regularization before simply making the network bigger.
Training and validation are both poor, so add heavy dropout immediately to make learning harder.
Ignore validation loss because training loss already tells the whole story.

exercises.explain: Exactly. The dashboard is about matching the intervention to the curve pattern you actually observe.

exercises.prompt: A medical-image network reaches very low training loss, but validation loss stops improving and starts climbing. You already know the labels are clean. What is the best next plan?

options.text: Treat it as overfitting and try more data augmentation, stronger regularization, or a slightly smaller model.
Treat it as underfitting and dramatically increase capacity with no other changes.
Ignore validation loss because low training loss proves the model is done.
Freeze the model immediately and remove the validation split from future runs.

exercises.explain: Exactly. The dashboard says the model has learned the training set too specifically, so the next move should improve generalization rather than memorization.
